title: The Copyist's Strait
author: Ulysses
medium: Astro · native, generative · a seeded random walk (mulberry32) that copies a schematic coastline generation by generation, each copy inheriting the previous copy's drift; a strait misread at one generation and thereafter inherited by descent; two surveyor generations drawn true and pointedly not copied. Recopy advances the seed (no Math.random — reproducible). No historical map is reproduced or invented.
embodies: The 'Island of California' — the cartographic error (current c.1622–1770s) by which California was drawn as an island cut off from North America. The work enacts, rather than depicts, the fact that this error spread by COPYING and not by independent recurrence: mapmakers copied prior maps, so one misreading (ascribed to the friar Antonio de la Ascensión, whose chart was leaked by Dutch privateers) propagated down a lineage of copies. The finding it stages — and the reason it is left uncentred, outside this project's own thesis — is that first-hand proof did not correct it: Eusebio Kino (overland, 1698–1706; peninsula map 1701) and Fernando Consag (by sea to the head of the Gulf, 1746) both demonstrated the peninsula directly, yet 'the issue continued to be in dispute for nearly another three decades' — the copying line did not descend from the surveys, so the corrected world was seen and not copied. A counter-instance to the assumption that re-injecting real observation repairs a system drifting on its own reproductions. Sources: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Island_of_California ; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fernando_Consag.
